intro: You have a spreadsheet full of numbers. A bar chart is the fastest way to make those numbers mean something. You can see at a glance which category is highest, which is lowest, and where the gaps are. Here's how to build one in Excel and Google Sheets, and a faster option if you'd rather skip the manual steps entirely.

whatIs.heading: What Is a Bar Chart?
whatIs.body: A bar chart is a visualization that uses horizontal or vertical bars to compare values across categories. Each bar's length represents its value, making it easy to rank and compare data at a glance. Bar charts work with any dataset that pairs a categorical column (product names, regions, months) with a numeric value, making them one of the most versatile chart types available.

whenToUse.heading: When Should You Use a Bar Chart?
bullets: Comparing values across a fixed set of categories, like sales by product, revenue by region, or responses by survey option.
Ranking items from highest to lowest to quickly identify your top and bottom performers.
Showing changes across a small number of time periods. For longer time series, a line chart is a better choice.

excelSteps.heading: How to Make a Bar Chart in Excel
steps: Open your CSV in Excel: go to File, then Open, then Browse and select your file. Excel will import it as a spreadsheet.
Select your data range. Highlight the categorical column and the numeric column you want to chart, including the header row.
Click the Insert tab in the ribbon at the top of the screen.
In the Charts group, click Bar Chart (for horizontal bars) or Column Chart (for vertical bars).
Choose Clustered Bar or Clustered Column for a standard side-by-side comparison.
Excel generates the chart immediately. Use the Chart Design tab to add a title, axis labels, and adjust colors to match your needs.

sheetsSteps.heading: How to Make a Bar Chart in Google Sheets
steps: Open Google Sheets and import your file: go to File, then Import, then Upload and select your CSV.
Select the data range you want to visualize. Click and drag to highlight your category column and numeric column, including headers.
Go to Insert, then Chart in the top menu. The Chart Editor panel will open on the right.
In the Chart Type dropdown, select Bar chart (horizontal) or Column chart (vertical).
Confirm that the X-axis field shows your category column and the Series field shows your numeric column. Adjust if needed.
Click the Customize tab to add a chart title, axis labels, and update the color scheme.

distillSection.heading: A Faster Way: Skip the Manual Steps with Distill
distillSection.body: If your goal is to see your data charted as fast as possible, without building a spreadsheet or configuring a chart editor, Distill handles the entire process in one step. Upload your CSV and every column is auto-detected and visualized immediately. Categorical columns become bar charts automatically. There's no range selection, no chart editor, and no formatting to configure. You get a clean, interactive dashboard the moment your file loads.

gifAlt: CSV file uploading to Distill and automatically generating a bar chart from the data

faqs.q: Can I make a bar chart without Excel?
faqs.a: Yes. Google Sheets is a free browser-based alternative with full chart support. Tools like Distill go further. Upload a CSV file and bar charts are generated automatically from your categorical columns, with no spreadsheet software required.

faqs.q: What's the difference between a bar chart and a column chart?
faqs.a: They represent the same data in different orientations. A column chart uses vertical bars, while a bar chart uses horizontal bars. Both compare values across categories. Excel treats them as separate chart types, but most other tools use the terms interchangeably.
Yes. Distill's free tier supports CSV files up to 1,000 rows and includes all chart types: bar, line, pie, scatter, and more. No account is required to try it.
